romania.title: Efectele vremii extreme în mai multe zone din țară. Imagini cu grindina care a lovit în Argeș / Persoană salvată de viitură cu elicopterul

Avertizările de vreme extremă sunt valabile și miercuri în patru județe ale țării și în zone din Buzău și Vrancea. În Dâmvovița, o persoană a fost salvată cu un elicopter SMURD, …

romania.title: Mesaj RO-Alert, după ce un porc mistreţ a fost văzut în Pipera-Voluntari, în zona unei școli
romania.summary: Autorităţile au transmis, marţi după-amiază, un mesaj RO-Alert după ce un porc mistreţ a fost văzut în zona Pipera-Voluntari, lângă Bucureşti. Animalul a fost observat în apropierea unei şcoli private din zonă, reprezentanţii acesteia dând asigurări că toţi elevii şi angajaţii sunt în siguranţă.

romania.title: Porc mistreț pe o stradă din Voluntari-Pipera. Mesaj Ro-Alert pentru populație

Oamenii au fost sfătuiți să evite zona, să rămână în locuinţe …

Vestea i-a bucurat pe mii de pasageri care riscau să fie condamnaţi să petreacă 2-3 ore în tăcere dacă uitau acasă stickul […] The post Veşti bune pentru românii care au uitat acasă stickul cu manele: Wizzair oferă internet în avion appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: Why eating in the middle of the night can cause gastrointestinal issues
medical.summary: Eating when the body is normally asleep appears to desynchronize the circadian clocks of different cell types in the intestines, a UT Southwestern Medical Center study suggests. The findings, published in PNAS, could help explain why shift work, jet lag and other environmental stressors that affect circadian rhythms are associated with irritable bowel syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, constipation and other gastrointestinal disorders.

medical.title: Nature-inspired toothpaste developed for sensitive teeth
medical.summary: A new toothpaste for relieving tooth sensitivity has been developed by UCL researchers using a nature-inspired material that supports bone regeneration.

medical.title: One tiny patch could bring hospital-style heart checks into homes
medical.summary: A lightweight wearable device developed by UNSW engineers could one day help people monitor their heart and breathing health from home, potentially reducing hospital visits and allowing doctors to detect problems earlier. The flexible sensor patch, which attaches to the chest or over peripheral arteries using medical adhesive tape, is designed to continuously capture subtle vibrations produced by the heart, lungs, blood flow and pulse waves.

medical.title: Keeping kids and canines safe together
medical.summary: A little over a year ago, about a dozen members of the Johns Hopkins Child Injury Prevention Network logged onto their monthly Zoom call. For those working in bustling emergency departments, the meeting is a chance to brainstorm ways to prevent the most troubling cases that come through their doors. A member of the group raised a virtual hand: "Can y'all do something about dog bites?"

medical.title: Novel therapy may reverse autism-related brain deficits, study suggests
medical.summary: Researchers have identified a promising new therapeutic strategy for autism spectrum disorder (ASD). A research team led by Director KIM Eunjoon of the IBS Center for Synaptic Brain Dysfunctions has now identified a promising new strategy for restoring NMDA receptor (NMDAR) function by targeting a glycine transporter called Slc6a20a/SLC6A20.

medical.title: Tiny molecular fix revived tuberculosis antibiotic candidate and led to two patents
medical.summary: How can we combat the growing global health crisis of antibiotic resistance? At Leiden University, researchers are tackling this issue from multiple angles. Ph.D. candidate Vladyslav Lysenko develops and redesigns new antibiotic molecules, while Sebastian Tandar studies how existing antibiotics can be used more effectively.

medical.title: New GLP-3 drug significantly slashes both weight and blood sugar levels in Phase III trial
medical.summary: Over the past few years, GLP-1 drugs have transformed the treatment landscape for obesity and type 2 diabetes. By mimicking a hormone that helps regulate blood sugar, slows digestion and curbs appetite, these medications have helped millions lose weight while improving metabolic health.

medical.title: Head cooling may reduce depression symptoms and change brain activity
medical.summary: Wearing a cooling cap for 30 minutes may improve a person's sense of well-being, according to a new study by Penn State researchers. In a recent publication in Acta Psychologica, the researchers demonstrated that head cooling may reduce depressive symptoms and alter the types of brain waves people produce. While no medical recommendations can be derived from this small, exploratory study, the results indicate head cooling may provide mental health benefits for the general population.

medical.title: AI could provide 'early alert' for breast cancer 6 years in advance
medical.summary: Three commercially available radiology AI systems have shown the potential to flag early signs of breast cancer up to six years before a diagnosis, according to a study published in Radiology.

medical.title: Exposure to moderate levels of air pollution may cause heart damage, study suggests
medical.summary: In one of the largest studies to date, researchers examined the relationship between long-term air pollution exposure and coronary atherosclerosis and found that even at moderate levels, long-term exposure to air pollution is associated with more advanced coronary artery disease. The research was published in Radiology.

medical.title: FDA approves Xocova for COVID-19 postexposure prophylaxis
medical.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Shionogi's Xocova (ensitrelvir), an oral antiviral, for postexposure prophylaxis (PEP) of COVID‑19 in adults and adolescents aged 12 years and older.

medical.title: A study of 8,300 older adults revealed a surprising salt habit
medical.summary: A large study of older adults in Brazil found that adding extra salt at the table is still a common habit, especially among men. While too much salt is linked to serious health problems and faster cognitive decline, researchers discovered that women’s salt-shaking habits were tied more closely to lifestyle and diet.

medical.title: Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report
medical.summary: Rising living standards, shorter working hours and a liveable climate are achievable, say the authors of a new study The post Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Scientists finally crack an “undruggable” pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
medical.summary: For decades, pancreatic cancer has been one of the most lethal cancers, with few effective treatment options. A new drug, daraxonrasib, targets the KRAS mutation that fuels most pancreatic tumors—something many scientists once thought couldn't be done. In a major clinical trial, the treatment nearly doubled survival for patients with advanced disease and reduced the risk of death by 60%.

medical.title: Scientists discover the master clock that controls biological growth and development
medical.summary: A newly discovered genetic clock acts as the master timekeeper for development, orchestrating crucial bursts of gene activity throughout a worm’s growth. When the clock is disrupted, development stops, offering fresh clues about how growth-related disorders may arise.

medical.title: What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more
medical.summary: New life-changing inventions, a global triumph for renewables, and England’s first Dutch-style ‘cycle street’, plus more The post What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic and Wegovy weight loss eventually plateaus
medical.summary: New NIH research reveals that semaglutide sparks different responses inside appetite-controlling brain cells, offering fresh insight into why GLP-1 weight-loss drugs don’t work the same for everyone. Scientists also found a possible way to extend the drugs’ effects, potentially helping patients push past weight-loss plateaus.

science.title: Oldest Maya Long Count calendar date may reveal how royalty turned time into power
science.summary: Archaeologists working at the ancient Maya site of El Palmar in Campeche, Mexico, have discovered what may be the earliest known Long Count calendar date in the Maya lowlands. It is carved into a stone monument and is interpreted as Aug. 31, AD 180, in our modern calendar. The finding could reveal insights into how the earliest Maya rulers used time to stake their claim to the throne.

science.title: 3D-printed nozzle array could streamline production of drug-delivery microparticles
science.summary: MIT researchers have demonstrated a low-cost design for specialized electronic nozzles, called triaxial electrospray emitters, that could be used to manufacture time-release drug-delivery particles or self-healing materials efficiently and at scale.

science.title: As Japan's popularity booms, a new survey shows strong anti‑foreigner sentiment
science.summary: Japan is experiencing historically high numbers of foreigners. Its population is shrinking, and its workforce is aging, driving foreign labor to historic levels.

science.title: Why are sloths slow? It's in their DNA
science.summary: Sloths are the slowest mammals on the planet, but living in dense jungles has made them notoriously difficult to study. For the first time, scientists have now sequenced and analyzed the two-toed sloth genome and revealed the genetics behind its extremely slow metabolism.

science.title: Where did language come from? Nobody really knows, but the theories are fascinating
science.summary: Humans are the only species known to use fully symbolic language: a system capable of expressing abstract ideas, imaginary worlds and endless combinations of meaning. But how did we get there?

science.title: How waves, ponds and green algae are accelerating sea ice melt in Antarctica
science.summary: Picture sea ice in your mind. You probably imagine brilliant white, snow-covered floes floating on the surface of the ocean, home to penguins in the south of the globe or polar bears in the north.

science.title: New dating of Spain's Sala Keimada rock art sanctuary reveals thousands of years of continuous use
science.summary: The Centro Nacional de Investigación sobre la Evolución Humana (CENIEH) has participated in a study published in the Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports on Sala Keimada, one of the rock art sanctuaries in Cueva Palomera, the main cave of the Ojo Guareña Karst Complex (Merindad de Sotoscueva, Burgos, Spain).

science.title: Savanna chimpanzees use tools for capturing and feeding on army ants, study shows
science.summary: Chimpanzees are the only great apes, apart from humans, that have adapted to living on savannas as well as in forests. However, it is not yet well understood how the harsh ecological conditions of the savanna—compared with those of the forest—affect the foods chimpanzees eat and how they obtain them. Now, a study led by the University of Barcelona and the Jane Goodall Institute Spain (IJGE) reveals for the first time the strategies savanna chimpanzees use to make tools and extract aggressive army ants—also known as marabunta—from their underground nests and eat them in these dry, hot habitats.

science.title: Ancient ground squirrel droppings reveal Arctic's rich evolutionary history
science.summary: Ground squirrel droppings, preserved for millennia in the Yukon's deep permafrost, have yielded an enormous amount of environmental DNA from dozens of species of plants, insects, microbes and large mammals, offering detailed genetic information about an environment that no longer exists. It is among the oldest ancient DNA ever recovered and sequenced.

science.title: 'Super-connected' teenagers key to tackling violent crime, study suggests
science.summary: Young people with the most connections to other suspected offenders of any age are almost five times more likely to carry knives than the average youth suspect, according to a new analysis of more than 200,000 U.K. police records. This top 5% of "super-connected" children aged 10–18 are almost three times more likely to commit violent crime, seven times more likely to commit robberies and nine times more likely to be involved in organized crime than the wider youth suspect population.

science.title: Teachers more likely to accept low AI grades than equivalent human grades, study finds
science.summary: Teachers were more likely to accept an overly harsh grade given to a student by AI than when the unduly low grade was handed out by a human. As AI is increasingly integrated into decision-making, concerns about AI errors are often countered by assurances that humans will oversee and check the algorithm's work.

science.title: These underwater 'living pink rocks' help store carbon: Scientists just found four new species
science.summary: Rhodoliths may look like small rocks on the seafloor, but they are actually living algae that create habitats for marine life and contribute to long-term carbon storage. A new study found that the deeper, low-light waters off Japan's Tanegashima Island harbor a surprisingly distinct and diverse community of these living pink rocks, including four species completely new to science.

science.title: Some drugs 'fail' because of unrealistic testing conditions, scientists discover
science.summary: A drug once dismissed as ineffective suddenly worked—when scientists tested it under more realistic conditions that mimic the human body. In this surprising new discovery, Northwestern University scientists uncovered a hidden rule of drug behavior. A medicine's effectiveness can change dramatically depending on the conditions inside our cells.

science.title: New maps chart old-growth forests across Alaska and British Columbia
science.summary: Mature and old-growth forests are vital for biodiversity, carbon storage, cultural traditions and economic activity. But in Alaska and British Columbia, these rich resources have not been reliably mapped, leaving much unknown about what land is protected. Now, University of Oregon researchers are leading a comprehensive mapping effort that sheds light on the location, makeup and conservation status of old-growth forests across the region.

science.title: After 20 years, scientists finally shrink a powerful laser onto a chip
science.summary: Researchers at EPFL have developed a chip-scale ultrafast laser that performs on par with traditional tabletop femtosecond lasers. The innovation could make advanced laser technologies far smaller, cheaper, and more accessible for applications ranging from medical diagnostics to atomic clocks.

science.title: Goethe never knew this 40-million-year-old ant was hidden in his collection
science.summary: Scientists examining amber from Goethe’s personal collection discovered three hidden fossil insects, including an extinct ant preserved in extraordinary detail. Advanced 3D imaging allowed researchers to see not only the ant’s outer features but also structures inside its body. The findings offer new clues about the species’ biology and suggest it likely built large nests in trees.

environment.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
environment.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

environment.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
environment.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

environment.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
environment.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

environment.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
environment.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

environment.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
environment.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

environment.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
environment.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

environment.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
environment.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

environment.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

mix_items.title: Mesaj RO-Alert, după ce un porc mistreţ a fost văzut în Pipera-Voluntari, în zona unei școli
mix_items.summary: Autorităţile au transmis, marţi după-amiază, un mesaj RO-Alert după ce un porc mistreţ a fost văzut în zona Pipera-Voluntari, lângă Bucureşti. Animalul a fost observat în apropierea unei şcoli private din zonă, reprezentanţii acesteia dând asigurări că toţi elevii şi angajaţii sunt în siguranţă.

mix_items.title: Porc mistreț pe o stradă din Voluntari-Pipera. Mesaj Ro-Alert pentru populație

Oamenii au fost sfătuiți să evite zona, să rămână în locuinţe …

Avertizările de vreme extremă sunt valabile și miercuri în patru județe ale țării și în zone din Buzău și Vrancea. În Dâmvovița, o persoană a fost salvată cu un elicopter SMURD, …

Vestea i-a bucurat pe mii de pasageri care riscau să fie condamnaţi să petreacă 2-3 ore în tăcere dacă uitau acasă stickul […] The post Veşti bune pentru românii care au uitat acasă stickul cu manele: Wizzair oferă internet în avion appeared first on Times New Roman .

Foarte mulți suspectează că avionul Nordis e un indiciu puternic că Marcel Ciolacu s-a înscris la Bacalaureatul din acest an. mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Oldest Maya Long Count calendar date may reveal how royalty turned time into power
mix_items.summary: Archaeologists working at the ancient Maya site of El Palmar in Campeche, Mexico, have discovered what may be the earliest known Long Count calendar date in the Maya lowlands. It is carved into a stone monument and is interpreted as Aug. 31, AD 180, in our modern calendar. The finding could reveal insights into how the earliest Maya rulers used time to stake their claim to the throne.

mix_items.title: 3D-printed nozzle array could streamline production of drug-delivery microparticles
mix_items.summary: MIT researchers have demonstrated a low-cost design for specialized electronic nozzles, called triaxial electrospray emitters, that could be used to manufacture time-release drug-delivery particles or self-healing materials efficiently and at scale.

mix_items.title: Why eating in the middle of the night can cause gastrointestinal issues
mix_items.summary: Eating when the body is normally asleep appears to desynchronize the circadian clocks of different cell types in the intestines, a UT Southwestern Medical Center study suggests. The findings, published in PNAS, could help explain why shift work, jet lag and other environmental stressors that affect circadian rhythms are associated with irritable bowel syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, constipation and other gastrointestinal disorders.

mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... mix_items.title: As Japan's popularity booms, a new survey shows strong anti‑foreigner sentiment
mix_items.summary: Japan is experiencing historically high numbers of foreigners. Its population is shrinking, and its workforce is aging, driving foreign labor to historic levels.

mix_items.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Nature-inspired toothpaste developed for sensitive teeth
mix_items.summary: A new toothpaste for relieving tooth sensitivity has been developed by UCL researchers using a nature-inspired material that supports bone regeneration.

mix_items.title: Why are sloths slow? It's in their DNA
mix_items.summary: Sloths are the slowest mammals on the planet, but living in dense jungles has made them notoriously difficult to study. For the first time, scientists have now sequenced and analyzed the two-toed sloth genome and revealed the genetics behind its extremely slow metabolism.

mix_items.title: Where did language come from? Nobody really knows, but the theories are fascinating
mix_items.summary: Humans are the only species known to use fully symbolic language: a system capable of expressing abstract ideas, imaginary worlds and endless combinations of meaning. But how did we get there?

mix_items.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
mix_items.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

mix_items.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
mix_items.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

mix_items.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
mix_items.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

mix_items.title: One tiny patch could bring hospital-style heart checks into homes
mix_items.summary: A lightweight wearable device developed by UNSW engineers could one day help people monitor their heart and breathing health from home, potentially reducing hospital visits and allowing doctors to detect problems earlier. The flexible sensor patch, which attaches to the chest or over peripheral arteries using medical adhesive tape, is designed to continuously capture subtle vibrations produced by the heart, lungs, blood flow and pulse waves.

mix_items.title: Keeping kids and canines safe together
mix_items.summary: A little over a year ago, about a dozen members of the Johns Hopkins Child Injury Prevention Network logged onto their monthly Zoom call. For those working in bustling emergency departments, the meeting is a chance to brainstorm ways to prevent the most troubling cases that come through their doors. A member of the group raised a virtual hand: "Can y'all do something about dog bites?"

mix_items.title: How waves, ponds and green algae are accelerating sea ice melt in Antarctica
mix_items.summary: Picture sea ice in your mind. You probably imagine brilliant white, snow-covered floes floating on the surface of the ocean, home to penguins in the south of the globe or polar bears in the north.

mix_items.title: New dating of Spain's Sala Keimada rock art sanctuary reveals thousands of years of continuous use
mix_items.summary: The Centro Nacional de Investigación sobre la Evolución Humana (CENIEH) has participated in a study published in the Journal of Archaeological Science: Reports on Sala Keimada, one of the rock art sanctuaries in Cueva Palomera, the main cave of the Ojo Guareña Karst Complex (Merindad de Sotoscueva, Burgos, Spain).

mix_items.title: Savanna chimpanzees use tools for capturing and feeding on army ants, study shows
mix_items.summary: Chimpanzees are the only great apes, apart from humans, that have adapted to living on savannas as well as in forests. However, it is not yet well understood how the harsh ecological conditions of the savanna—compared with those of the forest—affect the foods chimpanzees eat and how they obtain them. Now, a study led by the University of Barcelona and the Jane Goodall Institute Spain (IJGE) reveals for the first time the strategies savanna chimpanzees use to make tools and extract aggressive army ants—also known as marabunta—from their underground nests and eat them in these dry, hot habitats.

mix_items.title: Novel therapy may reverse autism-related brain deficits, study suggests
mix_items.summary: Researchers have identified a promising new therapeutic strategy for autism spectrum disorder (ASD). A research team led by Director KIM Eunjoon of the IBS Center for Synaptic Brain Dysfunctions has now identified a promising new strategy for restoring NMDA receptor (NMDAR) function by targeting a glycine transporter called Slc6a20a/SLC6A20.

mix_items.title: Ancient ground squirrel droppings reveal Arctic's rich evolutionary history
mix_items.summary: Ground squirrel droppings, preserved for millennia in the Yukon's deep permafrost, have yielded an enormous amount of environmental DNA from dozens of species of plants, insects, microbes and large mammals, offering detailed genetic information about an environment that no longer exists. It is among the oldest ancient DNA ever recovered and sequenced.

mix_items.title: Tiny molecular fix revived tuberculosis antibiotic candidate and led to two patents
mix_items.summary: How can we combat the growing global health crisis of antibiotic resistance? At Leiden University, researchers are tackling this issue from multiple angles. Ph.D. candidate Vladyslav Lysenko develops and redesigns new antibiotic molecules, while Sebastian Tandar studies how existing antibiotics can be used more effectively.

mix_items.title: New GLP-3 drug significantly slashes both weight and blood sugar levels in Phase III trial
mix_items.summary: Over the past few years, GLP-1 drugs have transformed the treatment landscape for obesity and type 2 diabetes. By mimicking a hormone that helps regulate blood sugar, slows digestion and curbs appetite, these medications have helped millions lose weight while improving metabolic health.

mix_items.title: 'Super-connected' teenagers key to tackling violent crime, study suggests
mix_items.summary: Young people with the most connections to other suspected offenders of any age are almost five times more likely to carry knives than the average youth suspect, according to a new analysis of more than 200,000 U.K. police records. This top 5% of "super-connected" children aged 10–18 are almost three times more likely to commit violent crime, seven times more likely to commit robberies and nine times more likely to be involved in organized crime than the wider youth suspect population.

mix_items.title: Head cooling may reduce depression symptoms and change brain activity
mix_items.summary: Wearing a cooling cap for 30 minutes may improve a person's sense of well-being, according to a new study by Penn State researchers. In a recent publication in Acta Psychologica, the researchers demonstrated that head cooling may reduce depressive symptoms and alter the types of brain waves people produce. While no medical recommendations can be derived from this small, exploratory study, the results indicate head cooling may provide mental health benefits for the general population.

mix_items.title: AI could provide 'early alert' for breast cancer 6 years in advance
mix_items.summary: Three commercially available radiology AI systems have shown the potential to flag early signs of breast cancer up to six years before a diagnosis, according to a study published in Radiology.

mix_items.title: Exposure to moderate levels of air pollution may cause heart damage, study suggests
mix_items.summary: In one of the largest studies to date, researchers examined the relationship between long-term air pollution exposure and coronary atherosclerosis and found that even at moderate levels, long-term exposure to air pollution is associated with more advanced coronary artery disease. The research was published in Radiology.

mix_items.title: Teachers more likely to accept low AI grades than equivalent human grades, study finds
mix_items.summary: Teachers were more likely to accept an overly harsh grade given to a student by AI than when the unduly low grade was handed out by a human. As AI is increasingly integrated into decision-making, concerns about AI errors are often countered by assurances that humans will oversee and check the algorithm's work.

mix_items.title: These underwater 'living pink rocks' help store carbon: Scientists just found four new species
mix_items.summary: Rhodoliths may look like small rocks on the seafloor, but they are actually living algae that create habitats for marine life and contribute to long-term carbon storage. A new study found that the deeper, low-light waters off Japan's Tanegashima Island harbor a surprisingly distinct and diverse community of these living pink rocks, including four species completely new to science.

mix_items.title: FDA approves Xocova for COVID-19 postexposure prophylaxis
mix_items.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Shionogi's Xocova (ensitrelvir), an oral antiviral, for postexposure prophylaxis (PEP) of COVID‑19 in adults and adolescents aged 12 years and older.

mix_items.title: Some drugs 'fail' because of unrealistic testing conditions, scientists discover
mix_items.summary: A drug once dismissed as ineffective suddenly worked—when scientists tested it under more realistic conditions that mimic the human body. In this surprising new discovery, Northwestern University scientists uncovered a hidden rule of drug behavior. A medicine's effectiveness can change dramatically depending on the conditions inside our cells.

mix_items.title: New maps chart old-growth forests across Alaska and British Columbia
mix_items.summary: Mature and old-growth forests are vital for biodiversity, carbon storage, cultural traditions and economic activity. But in Alaska and British Columbia, these rich resources have not been reliably mapped, leaving much unknown about what land is protected. Now, University of Oregon researchers are leading a comprehensive mapping effort that sheds light on the location, makeup and conservation status of old-growth forests across the region.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
mix_items.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

mix_items.title: After 20 years, scientists finally shrink a powerful laser onto a chip
mix_items.summary: Researchers at EPFL have developed a chip-scale ultrafast laser that performs on par with traditional tabletop femtosecond lasers. The innovation could make advanced laser technologies far smaller, cheaper, and more accessible for applications ranging from medical diagnostics to atomic clocks.

mix_items.title: A study of 8,300 older adults revealed a surprising salt habit
mix_items.summary: A large study of older adults in Brazil found that adding extra salt at the table is still a common habit, especially among men. While too much salt is linked to serious health problems and faster cognitive decline, researchers discovered that women’s salt-shaking habits were tied more closely to lifestyle and diet.

mix_items.title: Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report
mix_items.summary: Rising living standards, shorter working hours and a liveable climate are achievable, say the authors of a new study The post Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Scientists finally crack an “undruggable” pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
mix_items.summary: For decades, pancreatic cancer has been one of the most lethal cancers, with few effective treatment options. A new drug, daraxonrasib, targets the KRAS mutation that fuels most pancreatic tumors—something many scientists once thought couldn't be done. In a major clinical trial, the treatment nearly doubled survival for patients with advanced disease and reduced the risk of death by 60%.

mix_items.title: Goethe never knew this 40-million-year-old ant was hidden in his collection
mix_items.summary: Scientists examining amber from Goethe’s personal collection discovered three hidden fossil insects, including an extinct ant preserved in extraordinary detail. Advanced 3D imaging allowed researchers to see not only the ant’s outer features but also structures inside its body. The findings offer new clues about the species’ biology and suggest it likely built large nests in trees.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover the master clock that controls biological growth and development
mix_items.summary: A newly discovered genetic clock acts as the master timekeeper for development, orchestrating crucial bursts of gene activity throughout a worm’s growth. When the clock is disrupted, development stops, offering fresh clues about how growth-related disorders may arise.

mix_items.title: What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more
mix_items.summary: New life-changing inventions, a global triumph for renewables, and England’s first Dutch-style ‘cycle street’, plus more The post What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic and Wegovy weight loss eventually plateaus
mix_items.summary: New NIH research reveals that semaglutide sparks different responses inside appetite-controlling brain cells, offering fresh insight into why GLP-1 weight-loss drugs don’t work the same for everyone. Scientists also found a possible way to extend the drugs’ effects, potentially helping patients push past weight-loss plateaus.

mix_items.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
mix_items.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

mix_items.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
mix_items.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am facut buget pe luna asta. Primele doua zile au fost impecabile.

satire_ro.title: Ciolacu a demisionat din PSD și s-a declarat tehnocrat, iar Tomac l-a numit la Finanțe
